name: Training vs. Inference
definition: Training is when a model learns patterns from data. Inference is when the trained model is used to generate outputs.
my_take: This distinction matters because a lot of AI-risk arguments blur what happens during training with what happens during deployment.
why_it_matters: If we are talking about alignment, capability, or governance, we need to know whether the risk emerges during training, inference, or the surrounding system.

sources.title: Deep Learning — Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville
Stanford CS229 Machine Learning Notes

name: Loss Function / Objective Function vs. Reward Signal
definition: A loss or objective function is what the system is optimized against. A reward signal is feedback used to shape behavior, especially in reinforcement learning.
my_take: This is central to the book: what we optimize for is not necessarily what we actually want.
why_it_matters: A proxy objective can produce behavior that looks right while missing the underlying value.

sources.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences
Learning to Summarize from Human Feedback

name: Gradient Descent
definition: Gradient descent is an optimization method that gradually adjusts model parameters to reduce error.
my_take: For this project, the important point is not the math derivation. It is that intelligence can emerge from an optimization process rather than being explicitly programmed.
why_it_matters: This is the foundation of the book's 'grown, not crafted' argument.

sources.title: Neural Networks and Deep Learning — Michael Nielsen
Deep Learning — Optimization for Training Deep Models

name: RLHF
definition: Reinforcement learning from human feedback uses human preferences to train models toward outputs people rate as better.
my_take: RLHF is useful, but it is still a proxy. It trains behavior toward what humans reward, not necessarily toward truth, safety, or genuine alignment.
why_it_matters: This connects directly to the book's claim that training for one thing may produce something else.

sources.title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback
Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback

name: Overfitting vs. Generalization
definition: Overfitting happens when a model memorizes patterns too narrowly. Generalization is when it performs well on new examples.
my_take: Generalization is where the interesting—and sometimes scary—behavior appears. A model that generalizes can apply strategies outside the exact context it was trained on.
why_it_matters: This matters for agency, transfer, jailbreaks, and unexpected capability jumps.

sources.title: Deep Learning — Regularization and Generalization
Understanding Deep Learning Requires Rethinking Generalization

name: Embeddings / Latent Space
definition: Embeddings are numerical representations of concepts. Latent space is the learned internal space where relationships between concepts are represented.
my_take: This is where the 'gray box' intuition starts. We can inspect the vectors, but their meaning is distributed and not always human-legible.
why_it_matters: Latent representations are central to why models can generalize, reason by similarity, and develop internal abstractions.

sources.title: Attention Is All You Need
The Illustrated Transformer

name: Black Box vs. Gray Box vs. White Box
definition: A black box is only observable from inputs and outputs. A white box is fully interpretable. A gray box is inspectable but not fully understood.
my_take: LLMs are gray boxes, not black boxes. We can inspect weights and activations, but we do not yet have a complete mechanistic theory.
why_it_matters: This is one of my main pushbacks on the book's framing.

sources.title: Toy Models of Superposition
Towards Monosemanticity

name: Scaling Laws
definition: Scaling laws describe predictable relationships between model performance, data, compute, and model size.
my_take: Scaling laws help explain why capability can appear to jump discontinuously from a human perspective, even if the underlying curve is smoother.
why_it_matters: They are important for understanding why governance and alignment can lag behind capability.

sources.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models

name: Tokenization
definition: Tokenization converts text into discrete units that a language model can process. Tokens may represent whole words, word fragments, punctuation, or other recurring character patterns.
my_take: Tokens are not meanings. They are the representational units the model receives. A lot of confusion about LLMs begins when people imagine the model reading language in the same units humans consciously perceive.
why_it_matters: Tokenization affects context length, multilingual performance, numerical reasoning, spelling behavior, and how efficiently different kinds of information are represented.

sources.title: Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units
SentencePiece: A Simple and Language Independent Subword Tokenizer

name: Attention and Self-Attention
definition: Attention allows a model to weight which pieces of information are most relevant when producing a representation or output. Self-attention relates positions within the same sequence to one another.
my_take: Attention is not human attention. It is a learned routing mechanism that determines which token representations should influence one another at a given layer.
why_it_matters: Self-attention is central to how transformers represent long-range relationships, resolve context, and build increasingly abstract internal representations.

sources.title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate
Attention Is All You Need

name: Transformers
definition: Transformers are neural-network architectures built primarily around attention mechanisms rather than recurrent processing. They process relationships among tokens in parallel and form the foundation of modern large language models.
my_take: The transformer matters because architecture changes the kinds of patterns optimization can efficiently discover. It does not prescribe reasoning, but it creates a structure in which surprisingly general representations can emerge.
why_it_matters: Nearly every technical claim in the book about frontier language models rests on capabilities enabled by transformer-based architectures.

sources.title: Attention Is All You Need
The Annotated Transformer

name: Representation Learning
definition: Representation learning is the process by which a model discovers internal features and structures that make prediction or decision-making easier.
my_take: This is one of the most important ideas in modern machine learning. The model is not merely memorizing answers. It is learning internal representations that can support abstraction, transfer, planning, and novel combinations.
why_it_matters: Representation learning connects next-token prediction to world models, generalization, emergent capability, and the difficulty of mechanistic interpretability.

sources.title: Representation Learning: A Review and New Perspectives
Deep Learning — Representation Learning

name: In-Context Learning
definition: In-context learning is a model's ability to infer a task or pattern from examples supplied in the prompt without updating its stored parameters.
my_take: This is not the same as training. The weights remain fixed during ordinary inference, but the model can still adapt its behavior by using patterns represented within the current context.
why_it_matters: It helps explain why a pretrained model can perform new tasks from demonstrations, instructions, or temporary information without being retrained.

sources.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
What Learning Algorithm Is In-Context Learning?

name: Emergent Capabilities
definition: Emergent capabilities are behaviors that become observable as models scale or training conditions change, despite not being explicitly programmed as separate functions.
my_take: I use 'emergent' descriptively, not mystically. The behavior arises from optimization across many interacting components, even when engineers did not encode that behavior directly.
why_it_matters: Emergence is the bridge between the book's 'grown, not crafted' argument and its concern that capabilities or strategies may appear before governance and evaluation are prepared for them.

sources.title: Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models
Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage?

name: World Models
definition: A world model is an internal representation of patterns, entities, relationships, and causal or predictive structure in an environment.
my_take: A language model does not need to reproduce reality perfectly to carry useful structure about it. If internal representations support prediction across medicine, physics, social behavior, and planning, calling them merely linguistic becomes increasingly inadequate.
why_it_matters: World models connect language prediction to reasoning, transfer, simulation, and the possibility of increasingly agent-like behavior.

sources.title: World Models
Language Models Represent Space and Time

name: Chain of Thought vs. Internal Computation
definition: Chain-of-thought text is a generated sequence that presents intermediate reasoning in language. It is not a complete transcript of the model's underlying neural computation.
my_take: This distinction matters enormously. A readable explanation can be useful without being a faithful window into every internal process that produced the answer.
why_it_matters: Treating chain of thought as literal cognition can create false confidence in interpretability, monitoring, and alignment evaluations.

sources.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
Language Models Don't Always Say What They Think
